description.task: Build a simplified IoC container in Java that manages bean creation and retrieval. The container should store beans by type and resolve them on demand.
instructions: Implement registerBean(), getBean() (casting to type), and containsBean().
In Main, create the container and register UserService and EmailService as beans.
Print the welcome message and check if 'orderService' bean exists.
description.hint: Spring's ApplicationContext is an advanced IoC container. Beans are registered by the framework and resolved lazily or eagerly.
description.expectedOutput:
Welcome, Alice!
Email to bob@example.com: Hello
Bean 'orderService' exists: false

description.task: Demonstrate the difference between Singleton and Prototype bean scopes by implementing a simple scope-aware bean factory in Java.
instructions: Implement getSingletonCounter() to lazily create and reuse the same Counter instance.
Implement getPrototypeCounter() to always return new Counter().
In Main, get two singleton counters, increment both, and show they share state.
Get two prototype counters, increment each once, and show they have separate state.
description.hint: Spring default scope is singleton (one per container). Prototype creates a new bean each time — useful for stateful objects.
description.expectedOutput:
Singleton IDs: 1 and 1 (same instance)
Singleton count after 2 increments: 2
Prototype IDs: 2 and 3 (different instances)
Prototype counts: 1 and 1

description.task: Simulate how an IoC container resolves deep dependency chains. Build an object graph where OrderController depends on OrderService which depends on OrderRepository.
description.context: IoC containers resolve dependencies automatically. When you request a bean with deep dependencies, the container builds the entire object graph for you.
instructions: Implement OrderController.handleRequest() to print 'Handling order: [processed result]'.
Implement ApplicationContext.buildOrderController() to create all three beans and wire them together.
In Main, use ApplicationContext to get OrderController and call handleRequest(101) and handleRequest(202).
description.hint: This is exactly what Spring does when you call applicationContext.getBean(OrderController.class) — it builds the full dependency tree.
description.expectedOutput:
Handling order: Order#101 [PROCESSED]
Handling order: Order#202 [PROCESSED]
